title: Luiz Eduardo S. Oliveira
text: I earned my Ph.D. in Computer Science (Philosophiae Doctor Ph.D.) from the Université du Quebec, École de Technologie Supérieure in 2003. From 2004 to 2009, I served as a professor in the Graduate Program in Informatics at PUCPR. Since 2009, I’ve been a professor in the Department of Informatics at the Federal University of Paraná. Over the years, I’ve supervised and co-supervised more than 40 students and authored over 250 articles in specialized journals and international conference proceedings, boasting an h-index of 59. In 2003, I was honored with the excellence award for the best doctoral thesis from ETS/Université du Quebec, and in 2017, I received the IBM Faculty Award. My research focuses primarily on pattern recognition and machine learning. Since 2006, I have been a CNPq fellow.

title: Resumé
text:
I received the BS degree in computer science from the Faculdades Positivo (1995), Curitiba, PR, Brazil, MSc degree in Electrical Engineering and Industrial Informatics from the Federal University of Technology – Parana (UTFPR), Curitiba, PR, Brazil (1998), and Ph.D. degree from the Université du Quebec – Ecole de Technologie Superieure (ETS), Montreal, QC, Canada (2003). From 1994 to 1998 I was system analyst at HSBC Bank, Curitiba, PR, Brazil, where I worked on reengineering and financial systems. In 2000, I started to work in my PhD project in the Laboratory for Imagery, Vision, and Artificial Intelligence (LIVIA) in Montreal, under the supervision of Prof. Robert Sabourin from ETS and Prof. Ching Y. Suen from the CENPARMI, Concordia University. My thesis titled “Automatic Recognition of Handwritten Numerical Strings”, received the prize for the best 2003 Ph.D. thesis. The jury was composed of Prof. Tony Wong (ETS), Prof. Jacques de Guise (ETS) Prof. Ching Y. Suen (Concordia University), Prof. Yoshua Bengio (University of Montreal). From 2000 to 2003 I was also visiting researcher at CENPARMI.
From 2004 to 2005 I was visiting professor (posdoc supported by The National Council for Scientific and Technological Development, CNPq) at the Pontifical Catholic University of Parana, Curitiba, PR, Brazil. From 2005 to 2009 I was professor of computer science at the Pontifical Catholic University of Parana (PUCPR) and head of the Vision, Image, and Robotics research group. Currently I´m with the Department of Informatics of Federal University of Parana (UFPR), Curitiba, PR, where I’m head of the Graduate Program in Computer Science for the second term (2011-2014, 2017-2019). In 2015, I was invited professor at Université de Roeun, in France. I’m also an associated member of the LIVIA at ETS. Since 2006 I´m a CNPq researcher fellow (level 1D). In 2017 I received the IBM Faculty Awards for the work on texture classification. My research interests include pattern recognition and machine learning. For more details, visit my publications.
Since 2000 I have collaborated as program committee member and reviewer for several conferences and peer-reviewed journals. With some colleagues, we have organized some conferences Curitiba, Brazil (ISMIR, CBDAR, IAPR GREC, and IAPR ICDAR). In the recent years I have contributed as program Chair and competition chair in workshops and conferences such as ICFHR and ICDAR. Currently I’m associate editor for the IJDAR, ESWA, and SNCS journals.

title: Research on Texture Classification has been recognized
text: Very glad to announce that I have been selected for the 2017 IBM Faculty Award, which is a competitive worldwide program intended to foster collaboration between researchers at leading universities worldwide and those in IBM research, development and services organizations. Thanks to my colleague at IBM Research, Dr. Paulo Cavalin, for our fruitful collaboration over the years.

title: Dr. Teruo
text:
Yesterday, on July 19th, 2021, the doctoral defense of student Teruo Maruyama took place. In his thesis, Teruo investigated how to synthetically generate handwritten signatures to train robust machine learning models. The thesis titled “Automatic Intrapersonal Variability Modeling for Offline Signature Verification” will be available soon. A summary of the thesis can be found here. The examining committee was composed of the following professors:

Moises Dias (University of Las Palmas de Gran Canaria - Spain)
Prof. George Cavalcanti (UFPE)
Prof. David Menotti (UFPR)
Prof. Alceu Britto Jr (PUCPR)
Prof. Robert Sabourin (ETS - Canada)

And like everything we have been doing at the university since March 2020, this defense took place remotely.

title: Dr. Leandro
text:
On February 19th, Leandro Ensina’s doctoral defense took place. Leandro developed his doctoral thesis within the context of an R&D project financed by COPEL. The research focused on the use of dynamic regressor selection for fault localization in transmission lines. The examining committee consisted of professors Rafael Menelau Cruz from ETS/Canada, Huei Lee from Unioeste/PR, and Eduardo Almeida from DINF/UFPR. The most recent publication can be found here.

Considering that each member of the examining committee was located in a different part of the planet, the committee was conducted remotely.

title: Research
text:
My research interests are basically directed towards pattern recognition and machine learning. Here are some topics I’ve been working on the last 20 years
Handwriting recognition, segmentation, and verification. Signature and Writer Verification/Identification. Classification and Regression models (including deep learning models)  Dynamic Selection of Classifiers/Regressors. Concept Drift. Dissimilarity Pattern Recognition. Constrative Learning. Texture Classification.

title: #DeOlhoNosCorais project is in the media.
text: The Bori agency published an article about the work of master’s student Daniel Pimenta. Daniel worked on the #DeOlhoNosCorais project, which aims to classify coral images using deep learning. The project was developed in partnership with Professor Guilherme Longo from UFRN and had the support of the Serra Pilheira Institute. If you are interested in this project, check out this article published in PeerJ.

title: Scientists from UFPR are included in the list of top researchers in the world
text:
The Federal University of Paraná has 22 researchers listed in the 5th edition of the Updated science-wide author databases of standardized citation indicators, from Elsevier, related to the International Impact Ranking. The research includes the most influential scientists in the world in various areas of knowledge.
The ranking is divided into two categories - impact of research throughout the career or specific to previous years. Released at the beginning of October, the survey analyzes the authors’ performance through data from Scopus, recognized as one of the most important global repositories of abstracts and peer-reviewed scientific citations. Glad to be in both categories (at least for now).

title: Dr. Everton
text:
On December 3, 2024, Everton Baro defended his thesis. Everton began his work on predicting hospital admissions using health insurance data at the beginning of 2020, coinciding with the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic. Despite all the challenges, Everton achieved excellent results, which were published in a specialized journal in the field and presented at a conference. The thesis committee included Professors George Cavalcanti (UFPE), Julio Nievola (PUCPR), and Lucas Ferrari (UFPR). The work also benefited from the collaboration of Professor Alceu Britto Jr. from PUCPR. The most recent publication can be found here.

.
